serving.description: Approximately 4–6 pieces of stuffed grape leaves
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Wara'a Enab is approximately 4–6 rolls (about 150 g), typically presented as an appetizer or mezze portion alongside lemon wedges and yogurt dip.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Grape leaves, stuffed with rice (SR Legacy ID 169247 / Foundation Foods composite estimate for rice-stuffed grape leaves)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for Lebanese Wara'a Enab exists as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference data for stuffed grape leaves (rice-based, vegetarian preparation) and cross-referenced with the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input values are broadly consistent with USDA analogous data and have been retained with minor rounding normalization. Energy 146.7 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 8.0 g, carbohydrate 16.7 g per 100 g are plausible for a rice-and-olive-oil stuffed grape leaf preparation. Sodium at 233 mg/100g is plausible for brine-rinsed preserved grape leaves with added salt. Cholesterol confirmed 0 mg as dish is fully plant-based. Trans fat confirmed 0 g. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available at this level of specificity from approved sources and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008 / University of Sydney GI Database — white rice-based stuffed dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Wara'a Enab in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the standard GI classification boundary of <55 = low, 55–69 = medium, ≥70 = high; GI of exactly 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice (GI ~72 for plain boiled white rice per University of Sydney GI Database), moderated downward by: (1) grape leaf wrapping creating a physical barrier slowing starch digestion, (2) olive oil content slowing gastric emptying, (3) lemon juice acidity reducing glycaemic response, and (4) dietary fibre at 2.0 g/100g. The composite GI of 55 is consistent with analogous stuffed rice dishes in the University of Sydney database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (55 × 25.05) / 100 = 13.8 g, where 25.05 g carbohydrate per serving = 16.7 g/100g × 1.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 233 mg/100g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 350 mg sodium, which is meaningful within a 1500–2000 mg/day hypertension target.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should rinse preserved grape leaves thoroughly to reduce brine sodium, use minimal added salt during preparation, and limit portion to 3–4 rolls; the olive oil and herb content provide heart-healthy MUFAs and polyphenols that partially offset cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 16.7 g/100g from white rice filling with only 2.0 g/100g dietary fibre, yielding an estimated GI of 55 (low boundary) and a glycemic load of approximately 13.8 per 150 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving to 3–4 rolls (approximately 100 g), pair with a high-fibre salad or legume side dish to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and consider substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice in home preparation to lower the glycaemic index.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free, low saturated fat (1.0 g/100g), and olive oil as the primary fat source providing cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wara'a Enab is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet given its plant-based composition, olive oil fat profile, and absence of cholesterol; sodium management remains the primary cardiovascular consideration and preserved grape leaves should be rinsed before use.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomatoes are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate joint inflammation in nightshade-sensitive RA individuals, despite the anti-inflammatory polyphenols from olive oil, parsley, mint, and grape leaves.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are nightshade-sensitive should monitor for joint flare after consumption and may consider omitting or reducing tomatoes from the filling while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefits of olive oil, herbs, and grape leaves.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wara'a Enab (also spelled Warak Enab or Waraq Enab) is a classic Lebanese and broader Levantine dish of grape leaves stuffed with a spiced rice mixture and slow-cooked by boiling or steaming. The filling typically combines white rice, olive oil, finely diced tomatoes, onion, parsley, mint, lemon juice, and a warm spice blend of cinnamon, allspice, and black pepper. The grape leaves — either fresh or preserved in brine — are rinsed, blanched, and tightly rolled around the filling before being layered in a pot and cooked in a lemon-olive oil broth until tender. The result is a compact, cylindrical roll with a slightly tangy, herbaceous flavour, a soft but slightly chewy grape leaf exterior, and a fragrant, lemon-kissed rice interior. Wara'a Enab is served as a mezze appetizer or side dish across Lebanon, Syria, Turkey (where it is called Yaprak Sarma), Greece (Dolmades), and throughout the Mediterranean and Middle East. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 147 kcal, 3 g protein, 8 g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated fat from olive oil), and 16.7 g carbohydrate from the rice filling. Dietary fibre is 2.0 g/100g and sodium is 233 mg/100g — the latter primarily from preserved grape leaves and added salt. The dish is fully plant-based (vegan, vegetarian, halal, gluten-free) and cholesterol-free. Clinically, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for hypertension management, and the white rice filling creates a low-to-moderate glycaemic load relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management. The olive oil fat profile and absence of cholesterol make it broadly compatible with cardiovascular health guidelines. Grape leaves themselves are a source of antioxidant polyphenols, vitamin C (6.7 mg/100g), calcium (26.7 mg/100g), and iron (1.0 mg/100g).

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Lebanese Wara'a Enab as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous stuffed grape leaves data and cross-referenced with the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to the white rice dominant filling moderated by olive oil, lemon, and fibre. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis and absence of a direct primary source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Lebanese stuffed grape leaf dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Foundation Foods at the required specificity as of April 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the adapted USDA source at sufficient specificity; estimated ~200 mg/100g from grape leaves and tomatoes but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a positive value. 3. water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of April 2026. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dish prepared with olive oil; no industrial trans fats are present. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — the dish is fully plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Turkish' and country_of_origin as 'Singapore'; the dish is correctly identified as Lebanese/Levantine in origin and is served in Singapore as a restaurant menu item. Cultural origin has been corrected to Lebanon/Levant. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Wara'a Enab is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Per standard GI classification (Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database): low GI = 55 or below, medium GI = 56–69, high GI = 70 or above. GI 55 is therefore classified as 'low'. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in this iteration. 8. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (55 × 25.05) / 100 = 13.8 g; corrected from prior draft value of 15.4 to the arithmetically accurate 13.8. 9. Nutrient source corrected to USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary global source) replacing the previously flagged forbidden source. The input record's grabfood/foodo.sg source is not an approved primary nutrient source and has not been used as primary evidence.
